text: We present a case of a laparoscopic repair of a left psoas hernia with mesh.
The patient is a twenty four-year-old male presenting with a several week history of lifestyle limiting left abdominal and left flank pain.
The pain was exacerbated by movement.
He had no past medical or surgical history.
His physical exam was normal showing no hernias or other abnormalities.
A CT scan was obtained to further evaluate his pain.
CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a psoas hernia with his descending colon lying posterior to his left psoas muscle.
There were no signs of colon obstruction.
The CT was otherwise normal.
Given no other findings for his significant pain, a laparoscopic repair of the psoas hernia was recommended.
The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position, preparing for the laparoscopic repair of the psoas hernia.
The surgeon accesses the abdomen using a paraumbilical Hassan entry technique, preparing for laparoscopic repair of the psoas hernia.
Two additional 5mm ports are placed slightly left of midline, one in the subxiphoid area and the other in the lower midline, as visually demonstrated.
The laparoscopic view of the abdomen reveals the prominent left psoas muscle obstructing the visualization of the descending colon.
The surgeon uses retraction to elevate the descending colon from its position posterior to the prominent left psoas muscle, making the colon visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects along the white line of Toldt to medialize the descending colon, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The laparoscopic view shows the splenic flexure remaining in its original position, indicating that it has not been mobilized during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneum of the left lateral abdominal wall, carefully exposing the course of the nerves to avoid injury during the subsequent mesh fixation.
A 15x15 centimeter polyester composite mesh is positioned over the hernia defect in the abdominal wall.
The polyester composite mesh is secured to the lateral abdominal wall beneath the peritoneum using absorbable tacks.
The surgeon secures the polyester composite mesh medially to the anterior psoas and Dorotus fascia using a fibrin sealant, ensuring stable fixation of the mesh over the hernia defect.
The ureter was not covered.
The surgeon repositions the descending colon back into its anatomical location after mesh placement and dissection of the peritoneum.
The patient was discharged to home the day of surgery.
At follow-up, he reported resolution of his preoperative symptoms and had returned to normal activity.
